title: Sample creators will get payouts from Splice's new GenAI tools - Music Ally
text:
Splice is launching three new GenAI tools for musicians and producers, including one called ‘Variations’ that lets them make new versions of any sample in its catalogue by tweaking their structure, key and/or BPM.
However, it’s a sign of the times – an encouraging one – that the announcement of these tools focused just as much on the fact that the original creators of those samples will be getting paid “each time their sound is used as a source and each time the variation is downloaded”.
Variations is launching alongside ‘Craft’, a new feature for Splice’s ‘Instrument’ plugin, that turns samples into fully-playable instruments. This summer, they will be joined by the third new tool, ‘Magic Fit’, which “adapts any Splice sound to match the harmonic and rhythmic context of a session”.
With millions of musicians and producers using its service, Splice is at the sharp end of the opportunities
and
concerns around AI.
It knows it needs to be exploring what can be done with this technology, but equally it knows that it will be (rightly) slammed by its community if it does this in a way that seems exploitative and disrespectful.
“Producers have always used samples as a foundation for new ideas; these tools extend that tradition, enabling sounds to be reshaped and reimagined,” was how CEO Kakul Srivastava pitched the new launches.
“Grounded in deep, long-standing relationships with producers and sound creators, they reflect real creative workflows and reinforce the value of original work.”
Splice recently acquired startup Kits AI
as part of its expansion into AI tools, and has been
partnered with Universal Music Group since December 2025
to build “a roadmap for development of commercial AI tools rooted in creative control and sonic excellence”.
Indeed,
Srivastava appeared alongside UMG’s digital boss Michael Nash
at the HumanX AI conference this month, where ethical AI was a key talking point.
“I think the next generation of AI companies are fundamentally trying to do the right thing, where it comes to respecting artist rights and copyright and legal framework,” she said then.
“It’s not rocket science, and you really are seeing that the early days were different. It was a little bit Wild West, but we are one of many companies that are trying to do the right thing.”
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
